source_text: There's a bar in the square.

reference_translations.text: C'è un bar in piazza.
reference_translations.notes: Standard; new indefinite thing introduced with c'è.

reference_translations.text: In piazza c'è un bar.
reference_translations.notes: Place fronted; equally natural.

explanation: 'C'è un bar in piazza' introduces one new thing with the singular existential c'è (ci + è run together). Un bar is indefinite and new, so c'è, not plain è.

source_text: There are three eggs in the fridge.

reference_translations.text: Nel frigo ci sono tre uova.
reference_translations.notes: Standard; plural things take ci sono.

reference_translations.text: Ci sono tre uova nel frigo.
reference_translations.notes: Order variation, equally natural.

explanation: 'Ci sono tre uova' uses the plural existential because tre uova is plural. The existential esserci agrees with the things counted, so three eggs force ci sono.

common_errors.note: WRONG: tre uova is plural, so ci sono, not c'è.

source_text: In August there are a lot of tourists.

reference_translations.text: Ad agosto ci sono molti turisti.
reference_translations.notes: Standard; ad before agosto is common.

reference_translations.text: In agosto ci sono tanti turisti.
reference_translations.notes: tanti for molti, same sense.

explanation: 'Ci sono molti turisti' keeps the plural existential even though English says 'there is a lot of'. Italian counts the things: many tourists are plural, so ci sono.

common_errors.note: WRONG: molti turisti is plural, so ci sono.

source_text: There's no time.

reference_translations.text: Non c'è tempo.
reference_translations.notes: Standard negative existential; non before the cluster.

reference_translations.text: Non c'è più tempo.
reference_translations.notes: 'no more time' variant if that reading is meant.

explanation: 'Non c'è tempo' negates the existential by putting non in front of the whole cluster. The ci stays attached: non c'è, not non è.

source_text: There's nobody in the office.

reference_translations.text: Non c'è nessuno in ufficio.
reference_translations.notes: Standard; with nessuno the existential stays singular.

reference_translations.text: In ufficio non c'è nessuno.
reference_translations.notes: Place fronted, equally natural.

explanation: 'Non c'è nessuno' keeps the existential singular with nessuno, even though 'nobody' feels like many. Non goes before the cluster and the verb does not turn plural.

common_errors.text: Non ci sono nessuno in ufficio.
common_errors.note: WRONG: nessuno keeps the existential singular: non c'è nessuno.

source_text: Are there any questions?

reference_translations.text: Ci sono domande?
reference_translations.notes: Standard; plural things, plural existential.

reference_translations.text: Ci sono delle domande?
reference_translations.notes: With partitive delle, equally natural.

explanation: 'Ci sono domande?' asks about plural things, so the existential is plural ci sono. A common slip is c'è domande, which fails the number agreement.

source_text: There were two letters for you.

reference_translations.text: C'erano due lettere per te.
reference_translations.notes: Standard imperfetto; plural things take c'erano.

explanation: 'C'erano due lettere' uses the plural past existential: the imperfetto of esserci agrees in number, so two letters give c'erano, not c'era.

common_errors.note: WRONG: two letters are plural, so c'erano.

source_text: Yesterday there was an accident on this road.

reference_translations.text: Ieri c'è stato un incidente su questa strada.
reference_translations.notes: Standard; a completed event takes the passato prossimo c'è stato.

explanation: 'Ieri c'è stato un incidente' treats the accident as one completed event, so the existential is the passato prossimo c'è stato, built with essere. The imperfetto c'era would describe an ongoing background, not the one-off event.

common_errors.text: Ieri c'era un incidente su questa strada.
common_errors.note: WRONG here: a single completed event is c'è stato, not the ongoing c'era.

source_text: C'era una volta un piccolo villaggio in montagna.

reference_translations.text: Once upon a time there was a small village in the mountains.
reference_translations.notes: Standard; C'era una volta is the fairy-tale opening.

reference_translations.text: There once was a small mountain village.
reference_translations.notes: Natural compression.

explanation: C'era una volta is the fixed opening 'once upon a time there was'. The imperfetto c'era sets an ongoing past scene; English renders it 'there was'.

source_text: There's a book on the table.

reference_translations.text: C'è un libro sul tavolo.
reference_translations.notes: Standard; a new indefinite thing takes c'è.

reference_translations.text: Sul tavolo c'è un libro.
reference_translations.notes: Place fronted, equally natural.

explanation: 'C'è un libro sul tavolo' introduces a new, indefinite thing, so Italian uses the existential c'è. The calque 'Un libro è sul tavolo' wrongly treats the new book as a known subject to locate.

common_errors.note: WRONG: introducing a new indefinite thing takes c'è, not a fronted bare è.

source_text: The book is on the table.

reference_translations.text: Il libro è sul tavolo.
reference_translations.notes: Standard; a known, definite thing is located with plain essere.

explanation: 'Il libro è sul tavolo' locates a known, definite thing, so Italian uses plain essere with the book as subject. This is the mirror of the existential: c'è introduces new things, è places known ones. (Colloquially c'è il libro can mean 'the book is available', a different reading, so context decides.)

common_errors.note: WRONG as a locating sentence: a known, definite book is located with è; c'è il libro only works as a separate 'the book is available' reading.

source_text: In the centre there's a pharmacy. The pharmacy is on Via Roma.

reference_translations.text: In centro c'è una farmacia. La farmacia è in Via Roma.
reference_translations.notes: Standard; the pair shows the contrast: c'è introduces it, è then locates the now-known farmacia.

explanation: The two sentences show the whole contrast. Introducing the pharmacy for the first time (indefinite una farmacia) takes the existential c'è; once it is known (definite la farmacia), locating it takes plain è.

source_text: There are two mistakes in the text.

reference_translations.text: Ci sono due errori nel testo.
reference_translations.notes: Standard; new indefinite plural things take ci sono.

explanation: 'Ci sono due errori nel testo' introduces new, indefinite things, so the existential is used, plural ci sono. The bare-sono calque wrongly treats the new mistakes as a known subject.

common_errors.note: WRONG: new information is introduced with ci sono, not a fronted bare sono.

source_text: C'è una fermata dell'autobus proprio qui davanti.

reference_translations.text: There's a bus stop right here in front.
reference_translations.notes: Standard; c'è introduces a new indefinite thing, English 'there is'.

reference_translations.text: There's a bus stop just in front of here.
reference_translations.notes: Natural variant.

explanation: C'è introduces a new, indefinite thing (una fermata), so English is 'there is'. Reading it as the existential, not 'it is', keeps the sense of presenting something new.

source_text: La stazione è vicino al centro.

reference_translations.text: The station is near the centre.
reference_translations.notes: Standard; a known definite thing is located with essere, English 'is'.

explanation: La stazione is known and definite, so Italian uses plain è to locate it, and English is plainly 'is', not 'there is'. This is the è-side of the c'è vs è contrast.

source_text: Here's your coffee!

reference_translations.text: Ecco il tuo caffè!
reference_translations.notes: Standard; handing something over in the moment takes ecco.

reference_translations.text: Ecco a te il caffè!
reference_translations.notes: Colloquial variant with a te.

explanation: 'Ecco il tuo caffè!' presents something in the moment, so Italian uses ecco, not the existential c'è. Ecco points and hands over; c'è would merely report that coffee exists.

common_errors.note: WRONG for handing it over: that states the coffee exists somewhere, losing the here-you-go sense.

source_text: Non ti preoccupare: c'è un treno ogni mezz'ora.

reference_translations.text: Don't worry: there's a train every half hour.
reference_translations.notes: Standard; c'è states a recurring availability, English 'there's'.

reference_translations.text: Don't worry, there's a train every thirty minutes.
reference_translations.notes: Natural variant.

explanation: C'è states that something exists or is regularly available (a train every half hour), so English is 'there's'. Ecco would be wrong here: it points at a train arriving now, not a timetable fact.

source_text: Che c'è? Ti vedo preoccupato.

reference_translations.text: What's wrong? You look worried to me.
reference_translations.notes: Standard; Che c'è? idiomatically asks what the matter is.

reference_translations.text: What's up? You seem worried.
reference_translations.notes: Natural variant.

explanation: Che c'è? looks like 'what is there?' but as a fixed expression it means 'what's wrong / what's up'. Reading it literally misses the everyday sense.

source_text: Grazie mille per l'aiuto! Non c'è di che.

reference_translations.text: Thanks a lot for the help! Don't mention it.
reference_translations.notes: Standard; Non c'è di che is the set reply to thanks.

reference_translations.text: Thank you so much for the help! You're welcome.
reference_translations.notes: Natural variant.

explanation: Non c'è di che is a fixed reply to grazie, 'don't mention it / you're welcome'. It is built on esserci but cannot be taken apart literally.

source_text: Ti rispiego dall'inizio. Ci sei?

reference_translations.text: I'll explain again from the start. Are you with me?
reference_translations.notes: Standard; Ci sei? checks the other person is following.

reference_translations.text: Let me explain from the beginning again. Following me?
reference_translations.notes: Natural variant.

explanation: Ci sei? literally 'are you there?' is the fixed way to ask 'are you with me / do you follow?' while explaining. The idiom is on esserci, so learn it whole.

source_text: In the cappuccino there's milk and coffee.

reference_translations.text: Nel cappuccino ci sono latte e caffè.
reference_translations.notes: Standard; two things joined by e take the plural ci sono.

explanation: 'Ci sono latte e caffè' uses the plural existential because two things joined by e count as more than one, even though latte and caffè are each uncountable singulars.

common_errors.text: Nel cappuccino c'è latte e caffè.
common_errors.note: WRONG: latte and caffè joined by e count as more than one, so ci sono.
